Bearing 315 - May 1997

The Newsletter of Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest
May 1997

Cascade to Host 1998 Individual and Relay Champs and Eric Bone on the 1997 US Team!

COC to host 1998 Champs!

"The USOF Board Of Directors has granted Cascade Orienteering Club the 1998 Individual and Relay Championships. The vote on granting COC the two-day champs (the original bid) was 14-0-0. A motion was then made to amend this to be a one day individual and a relay. After some debate/discussion, this was passed with a vote of 11-3-0. Total elapsed time was just 35 minutes." [from the o-net, Editor]

Eric Bone on US Team

Eileen Breseman

[Image: A seal-like graphic saying "PRESS RELEASE"]
Eric Bone, of Seattle, Washington was one of five members selected to the U.S. Orienteering Team at the national trials held near Cincinnati, Ohio this past weekend. Bone, 22 years old, a UW senior and Garfield High graduate competed successfully by navigating the forest with map and compass through numerous checkpoints at top speed, to emerge in the top five positions. Two separate races in varied hilly terrain were totalled for both the Friday and Saturday meet.

Eric has competed for the U.S. two years ago at the '95 World Championship in Germany. He hopes to use his elite experience and this summer's advanced training in Sweden to advantage, when he represents the U.S. again at the '97 World Championships in Norway this August. He is a member locally of Cascade Orienteering Club, Seattle.

Pacific Northwest's own Fred Veler, U.S. Team Coach will accompany the team.

BullFrog Event - May 3,4

Dave Enger

[URGENT] The Sammamish Orienteering Club will be hosting a class B regional orienteering event on the Bullfrog areas near Roslyn and Cle Elum, Washington on Saturday and Sunday, May 3rd and 4th. Four courses will be available each day, ranging from beginner to advanced. Instruction will be provided, and all orienteers are welcome. Saturday's courses will be set on the Bullfrog Pond map by Mark Howlett. Sunday's courses will be set on the Bullfrog Flats map by Jan Urban.

Registration and starts will be from 11:00 AM to 1:00 PM on Saturday, and from 10:00 AM to noon on Sunday. From I-90, take Exit 80 (Roslyn, Salmon La Sac) and drive north on Bullfrog Road toward Roslyn for nearly two miles. Where the big powerlines cross Bullfrog Road, turn right. Parking, registration, starts and finishes will be at or near the powerline clearing. The event cost each day is $5.00 for orienteering club members, $6.00 for non-members, and $4.00 for those under 19 years of age.

Camping and cabins will be available for Saturday night at Camp Koinonia, located about four miles west of the event site. From I-90, take Exit 78 (Golf Club Road), drive south about half a mile, turn right onto Nelson Siding Road, drive west about half a mile, turn left into the Camp Koinonia driveway (a dirt road through the woods), and follow the driveway about a half mile to the camp. Camping cost per night is $5.00 for those 13 years or older, $2.50 for ages 9-12, and free for those under nine years of age. Cabin costs per night are double the camping costs ($10.00 for 13 years or older, $5.00 for ages 9-12). For more information on the event or accommodations, cal Dave Enger at 425-822-6254.

WSOA Meeting - May 3

Dave Enger

The Washington State Orienteering Association will meet on Saturday, May 3 at 4:00 PM at Kent Verbeck's home located between Easton and Cle Elum, Washington. The meeting will follow the Bullfrog Pond event earlier in the day. Kent will prepare one of his famous spaghetti feeds for meeting attendees. For more information about the meeting, call Kent Verbeck at 509-674-2755 or Dave Enger at 425-822-6254.

Washington State Champs at Trollhaugen

Mike Schuh

State Championships an A meet.
On August 23-24, Cascade OC will host a two day A meet at Trollhaugen (please see entry form in the July newsletter). The designation "A meet" means that the event will be organized to the standards of the US Orienteering Federation, and will include courses for all levels of experience, from novice to elite. For members of Washington State O' clubs, the event will serve as the 1997 state championships.

The format of the weekend event will be the same as that to be used in August 1998, when Cascade OC will host the US Championships. The first day will be a "normal" set of courses, and the individual results for the weekend will be determined by them. The second day will be a four-person relay, and will be the state relay championships. The courses that form the four laps of the relay will range from a beginner course to short advanced.

Eligible relay teams must have at least four "points" as follows: participants 35 years old or older earn 1 point, 50 and older earn 2 points, 65 and older earn 3 points, 20 and younger earn 1 point, 16 and younger earn 2 points, and 14 and younger earn 3 points (age is based on the participant's age as of December 31, 1997); women contribute an additional 2 points. Teams with 4 points comprised of four different members of the same Washington State O' club will be eligible for the state relay championship; awards will be given to other 4 point teams. Participants are welcome to form teams that do not have 4 points, and participants will be allowed to do more than one lap of the relay (e.g., two participants could do the laps as person A, person B, person A, person B), but these teams will not receive awards.

From I-90 exit 62, head southwest and west, following orange and white O' signs to the start/finish area. There will be very limited facilities.

For questions or to volunteer, please call Mike Schuh 206-783-3960.

What's An Orienteering Convention?

Rick Hood

This year on August 23/24, Cascade Orienteering Club is hosting an "A"-meet on the Trollhaugen map just off of I-90 some 62 miles east of Seattle. (Please see Meet Director Mike Schuh's article in this issue and the registration flyer at upcoming local events and the next issue of the Bearing.) COC is also hosting the annual United States Orienteering Federation Convention starting that Sunday afternoon, the 24th, and continuing through Tuesday the 26th.

Since many of you may not be familiar with what happens at a USOF convention or why it is an enjoyable and important event, I thought you might appreciate a little more info. (We will have all of the details in the next issue, but please feel free to give me a call; Rick at 367-2606 in Seattle, or visit our website http://weber.u.washington.edu/~bill3/conv97.html if you have any questions before then.)

Basically, the convention is a blend of three things: workshops, meetings/discussions, and social orienteering events and programs. The two days are also dynamic and fun. So whether you are involved with helping run your club, or a meet, or would just like to run better at a meet, you will find the convention the "place to be".

This year's "formal" workshops will have a broad range from GPS for O Mapping to O First Aid 101 (ankles to Zinc Oxide) for example; they will also be mostly hands-on or interactive. The workshops will be offered Monday morning, Monday afternoon, and Tuesday morning. Moreover, because there are about three different workshops run concurrently, there is truly something for everyone. There will also be a guided hike or alternative activity during these periods. (For example, we have arranged to have the author of the climbing guide for the local area to come and give a brief talk and then host a session "on the rock" for those wishing to try "vertical O".)

Many come to the convention also to take an active part in USOF and attend some of committee meetings that really affect all of us. These range from rankings (the good, the bad, and the ugly) to environmental concerns (orienteering land access 101). And while these might sound somewhat daunting or dry, each host really goes out of his or her way to make everyone, especially newcomers, feel welcome and keep the meeting moving. So, here is your chance to help your club and USOF grow; moreover, you can really get the lowdown on what is what or likely to be such, as well as let the folks upstairs know what you think. (These shorter/smaller sessions are scheduled for late afternoon and early evening.)

There are also those who feel that the heart of the convention is the socializing with orienteers from around the nation. To promote that we will have both informal and "formal" social options. For example, there will be Peter Golde modified score-O events before lunch on Monday and Tuesday and the first ever "regional volleyball tournament" in the late afternoons. We will also have more "formal" social programs in the evenings. All of this, and more, will be detailed in the next issue of the Bearing: for those in training for the annual 2-mile run, the course this year is both flat, fast, and easy to follow (early odds makers put Mark Howlett at?. no wait, I'd better not say).

All this takes place about 34 miles east of Seattle at a scenic environmental camp just outside of North Bend. You can stay at the camp or commute from home or go up-scale and stay at a motel in the area. The camp has arranged a nice set of meals for us, too. (Besides, everyone knows that the really good times and info are had at the meals; did I mention door prizes.) Mostly, we think you will like the nice workshop and meeting rooms coupled with a rich trail network big enough for the score-O events right at camp.

OK, just wanted to give you a heads-up on why you really should take Monday the 25th and Tuesday the 26th off from work and plan on coming to the USOF Convention that is this year in our own backyard. So don't miss the fun, the fellowship, and the field sessions: save those dates; you will be glad you did.

Mappers Wanted

Debbie Newell

Teanaway Forks and Lick Creek
The Cascade Orienteering Club (Seattle, WA) is accepting bids for field checking and cartography for two orienteering maps: Teanaway Forks and Lick Creek.
Location: North of Cle Elum, approximately 85 miles east of Seattle
Size: Total area to be mapped is 20 sq. km.
Teanaway Forks - 15 sq. km
Lick Creek - 5 sq. km
Final scale: 1:15000, 5m contour interval
Base map provided: Photogrammetric base map by Ivar Helgesen
Food and lodging: Provided
Transportation allowance: Up to $200
Cartography shall be done in OCAD 5.0.

Mapping schedule: Begin fieldwork August 1, 1997; complete fieldwork & begin cartography, September 15, 1997; and complete cartography October 15, 1997

You may submit a bid for only one element of the project, i.e. cartography only or fieldwork only. Please submit your bid by May 15, 1997. Include your name, address, phone number, price, qualifications, list of previous successful projects, and up to 3 references (include name and phone number). Please express payment in U.S. dollars per square kilometer, with separate amounts for each element. Submit your bids to:
Cascade Orienteering Club
Mapping Bids
P.O. Box 31375
Seattle WA 98107

Farrel-Mc Whirter
The Cascade Orienteering Club is looking for volunteer mappers to update our orienteering map of Farrel-McWhirter Park in Redmond. The project includes converting the map to OCAD 5.0. If you are interested in this project, contact Debbie Newell at 206-488-7471.

US Team Trials

Eileen Breseman

Hi out there. Just though you NW o'ers would like some fast breaking news of the past weekend.

Team Selection was as follows: Peggy Dickison, Kris Harrison, Sharon Crawford, - Discretionary picks: Kristin (Federer) Hall, and Karen Williams.
Alternates based on 6 preselected races in '96 and the 2-day Team Trials: #1 Penny DeMoss (not at the trials), #2 Debbie Newell, #3 Eileen Breseman.

For the men: James Scarborough, Mihai Veres*, Peter Anderson - Discretionary picks: Mikell Platt, Eric Bone. Alternates: Gregory Balter*, Eirik Moe, Michael Eglinski, and Mark Everett. (* pending IOF approval)

Debbie had a great race going on Friday, but accidentally skipped a control (discovered after finishing). Eileen came in 5th, 3 minutes behind 3rd (the position needed to gain a team position). On Saturday's classic distance Debbie altruistically stopped helped a grandmotherly type find her directions, losing some minutes. Eileen came in 4th, with Crawford only 1 1/2 minutes behind, losing a position literally by minutes. Eric placed 5th and 2nd I think for Fri & Sat respectively.

Sunday was a motala (2 loops with map exchange in the middle, 3 loops for the M21, - 12 km., with same leg controls and forked legs. F21 was 7.8k and a tiring one at that for sore, hill weary legs. Debbie took an overall 2day 1st place for the A meet. Eric took 2nd in the mens. Eileen finished!

Also ran: Bruce McAlister on Sat & Sunday, helping out on Friday. Fred Veler on Green Friday & Saturday, sore ankle keeping him out of competition on Sunday.

Finn Hill Pot-Luck

Bruce McAlister

Finn Hill Pot-Luck - Wednesday, June 11
Return to Finn Hill Park. The map has been updated, and gets a little better technically, but the green is still green. Part of the Wednesday evening schedule this year. Three courses: Novice, Intermediate, and Advanced. The course starts and ends at Bruce McAlister's, just east of St. Edward. There will be a pot luck and barbecue to relax after the meet.

Orienteering In Europe

Michel Billaux

If you want to go to Europe this summer, don't miss the Junior World Orienteering Championships (JWOC) in Belgium on July 4-11 1997. Spectator races are organized the same week in Belgium and in the Netherlands:
July 4: in the Netherlands
July 5: in the Netherlands
July 6: in the Netherlands
July 7: at Lichtaart in Belgium
July 8: at Lichtaart in Belgium
July 9: at Leopoldsburg in Belgium
July 10: at Leopoldsburg (short distance)
July 11: at Leopoldsburg (classic courses)

You will have the possibility to be invited in Belgian families and to participate to social events. It is also possible to combine tourism and orienteering. Leopoldsburg is located at 40 miles from Brussels, 80 miles from the medieval city of Brugge, and at only 210 miles from Paris. You will receive all the necessary support from the Belgian orienteers to organize your trip. All the orienteers of the Northwest are welcome. You will find more detailed information on the web: http://www.ping.be/%7Eping0623/jwoc/index.htm For more information, for registration to the courses, and to be introduced into a Belgian family, please contact Michel Billaux, 9404 W.Court St., Pasco, WA 99301, tel: (509) 545-8036 (home) or (509) 375-8130 (work), e-mail: billaux@3-cities.com (home) or mrb@nfuel.com.

Cle Elum Ridge Event

Mike Schuh

Even will use a different start/finish area!
The two day orienteering event at Cle Elum Ridge June 14-15 will feature courses designed by Jim Siscel and Eric Bone and will use a different start and finish area (not the usual one). Through the courtesy of George and Penny Blackburn, who own some of the land on the map, we will have access to an area towards the west end of the map. For most of the courses (mostly the shorter ones), this will provide a fresh experience, using terrain that is rarely used by anything but the longer courses. Jim and Eric are designing some good courses - come and take advantage of this opportunity to see "new" areas of an old map.

There are no facilities; bring extra drinking water.

From I-90 exit 85, go east on SR 970 about 3 miles, turn north on Masterson Road, and then east 1.5 miles on Red Bridge Road, and turn north up Whiel Road. At the two gates, bear left and follow orange and white O' signs to the start area. Start times are the usual 10:00 AM until noon. For questions or to volunteer, please call Mike Schuh 206-783-3960.

Field Notes

Rick Hood

St. Edward State Park
On Saturday the 10th of May and Sunday the 8th of June, COC will be hosting work parties at St. Edward State Park, the home of our annual Bog Slog (a.k.a. O' Lang Syne) event. Our project for this year is to "attack the green". By that I mean we have been asked to help remove unwanted non-native vegetation. So, rather than building trail, this year we will be making the area more runable. (OK, the park isn't actually taking this tack for our benefit, but rather to keep the non-native vegetation in check. Say, are blackberries native?)
Please call (Rick) at 367-2606 if you are planning to help out, as the ranger staff would like to assign us an area relative to our "volunteer size".

EarthWork/Earth Day
Four COC members (Nic Bone, Don Denbo, Jim Siscel, and Rick Hood) and Dave Tallent from SAMM helped out with trail work in conjunction with EarthWork/Earth Day at various projects. The one the majority of us worked on was a small non-orienteering park but also one in the same system as both Hamlin and Boeing Creek. In short, the King County Parks Dept. was impressed with our work and, on top of the satisfaction we received for our efforts, the projects help foster/maintain a positive rapport with the Parks Office. Next year, Rick suggested we do a project in Hamlin, Boeing Creek with KC or one at Woodland or Seward with Seattle Parks Dept.; any interest?

Moran State Park
Speaking of state parks, Rick Hood went to a meeting about user access regarding off-trail areas at Moran State Park, since we have a map of Moran and have experienced some access problems in the past. Basically, there are three issues: How orienteering is classified in the State Park's CAMP or Classification and Management Planning project; how the land at Moran is classified; and what precedent, if any, this has for other State Parks and Washington State public lands. (Rick spoke with three key individuals from the Department and followed up our concerns with a multi-page letter; he hopes to be able to report more positive new in the next Bearing. This is a potentially very major issue for all orienteers and we thus welcome your input and involvement.)

The Orienteering Hotline
You might notice some "new voices" on the orienteering hotline - always a great source for the latest O updates at 783-3866 in Seattle - as we are actively seeking to have the meet directors update mailbox number three, which has the details about the next local event. Our hope is that this will get you the best and most accurate info. (The general info on mailbox number one and the schedule-in-brief on mailbox two are being done by Margie Bone and Rick Hood, respectively, though if someone else would like to jump in...)

Bearing 315 Editor Change
Thanks to Don Denbo, who has stepped forward to put out this and the next issue of the Bearing. (If we are nice to Don, he may even continue being the editor.) You may also notice that the Bearing is being mailed first class again as we felt you are more interested in a timely than a voluminous newsletter; are we right? (Let Don know.)

Nic Bone to Sing!

On the lighter side. Want to hear O' done musically; come hear Nic Bone in "Oklahoma" this month in one of the UW productions. (Please wear your best O suit or other formal garb for preferred seating; for more info, call Nic at 634-2279; no autographs please.)

Thanks for the Help

A special thanks to Mike and Vivien Fritz and Ron Brown for agreeing to serve as Cascade's "Map Bid Evaluation Committee" which the club has implemented in an effort to more effectively explore and evaluate the mapping projects bids for our '97 and '98 projects.

Late Breaking News

Late breaking news: Congratulations to the winning team in the mixed division of the Twenty Mile Marysville-Pilchuck Spring Relay. The 9 orienteers each ran 2 miles legs to easily out pace about 10 other teams. Team members Paul Magnus Rick and Eileen Breseman, Keene Williams, and Debbie Newell of Cascade and Chris Lum, Mark Howlett, and Jana & Jan Urban of SAMM each will take turn housing the team trophy! Oh, and since putting the team together was Debbie's idea, they made her run two legs. Debbie also put together a team for the xc ski, downhill ski, canoe, bike, and run multi-event Ridge-to-River Relay and was joined by again by Chris and Jan. She also recruited Kevin Welton of COC and Samm's Dave Tallent. Now they didn't exact win, but they had a strong finish and came back with a story that will be extremely funny in 15 or so years.

Fee in the Forest

As you may know, come June 1st the US Forest Service will implement a "Trail Park" Pass System, very similar to the existing "SnoPark" System. The bad news is that one must pay $3 a car to park within a 1/4 mile of trailhead at selected locations or obtain a yearly pass for $25. The good news is that at least 80% of the monies generated must be used for maintenance of trails in that area. The better news is that if one is willing to volunteer two days doing trail work on USFS lands, one gets a pass for those work days plus a yearly pass for no cost. The best news for orienteers is that since we don't typically use a designated trailhead parking area, the Trail Park System only effects us if we are going hiking or some other non-orienteering activity.

New Web sites announced

We are pleased to pass on a new orienteering website from Olive Kearney of LAOC that has schedule info for all of the west coast. The address is: http://www.geocities.com/Yosemite/Trails/4040/. (Of course, Howard Halter's, of Sammamish OC, website is a super place for puget sound info. The address is: http://www.eskimo.com/~halter/orienteering/pnw.html.)

Congrats Eric!

And last by not least, congrats to COC's Eric Bone, who took 2nd place at the orienteering team trials and is again on our National Federation Orienteering Team. Eric will be join by 9 others from around the country for the upcoming international federation competition, all under the seasoned eye of USOF Team Coach - our own Fred Veler of Sammamish OC.

What a wonderful day to orienteer. It was 70F and severely clear over the course at the confluence of the mighty Snake and Columbia Rivers. The course was set over a wonderful site to observe wildlife, which could explain some of the times that were turned in. One pair of competitors was nearly lapped by an ambitious runner who covered the advanced course twice in succession.

Coyotes were seen (and heard), as were pheasant, deer, ducks, quail, and red-tailed hawk. A real birder could report many more. Hmmm... a new form of Score-O? Five points for a control, one point for each type of bird identified???

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently adminis-tered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteer-ing is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

What equipment do I need?

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protrac-tor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for loan (with deposit) at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense under-brush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair of socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

What is USOF?

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!

Cascade to Host 1998 Individual and Relay Champs and Eric Bone on the 1997 US Team!

"The USOF Board Of Directors has granted Cascade Orienteering Club the 1998 Individual and Relay Championships. The vote on granting COC the two-day champs (the original bid) was 14-0-0. A motion was then made to amend this to be a one day individual and a relay. After some debate/discussion, this was passed with a vote of 11-3-0. Total elapsed time was just 35 minutes." [from the o-net, Editor]

[Image: A seal-like graphic saying "PRESS RELEASE"] Eric Bone, of Seattle, Washington was one of five members selected to the U.S. Orienteering Team at the national trials held near Cincinnati, Ohio this past weekend. Bone, 22 years old, a UW senior and Garfield High graduate competed successfully by navigating the forest with map and compass through numerous checkpoints at top speed, to emerge in the top five positions. Two separate races in varied hilly terrain were totalled for both the Friday and Saturday meet.

Eric has competed for the U.S. two years ago at the '95 World Championship in Germany. He hopes to use his elite experience and this summer's advanced training in Sweden to advantage, when he represents the U.S. again at the '97 World Championships in Norway this August. He is a member locally of Cascade Orienteering Club, Seattle.
Pacific Northwest's own Fred Veler, U.S. Team Coach will accompany the team.

[URGENT] The Sammamish Orienteering Club will be hosting a class B regional orienteering event on the Bullfrog areas near Roslyn and Cle Elum, Washington on Saturday and Sunday, May 3rd and 4th. Four courses will be available each day, ranging from beginner to advanced. Instruction will be provided, and all orienteers are welcome. Saturday's courses will be set on the Bullfrog Pond map by Mark Howlett. Sunday's courses will be set on the Bullfrog Flats map by Jan Urban.

Registration and starts will be from 11:00 AM to 1:00 PM on Saturday, and from 10:00 AM to noon on Sunday. From I-90, take Exit 80 (Roslyn, Salmon La Sac) and drive north on Bullfrog Road toward Roslyn for nearly two miles. Where the big powerlines cross Bullfrog Road, turn right. Parking, registration, starts and finishes will be at or near the powerline clearing. The event cost each day is $5.00 for orienteering club members, $6.00 for non-members, and $4.00 for those under 19 years of age.

Camping and cabins will be available for Saturday night at Camp Koinonia, located about four miles west of the event site. From I-90, take Exit 78 (Golf Club Road), drive south about half a mile, turn right onto Nelson Siding Road, drive west about half a mile, turn left into the Camp Koinonia driveway (a dirt road through the woods), and follow the driveway about a half mile to the camp. Camping cost per night is $5.00 for those 13 years or older, $2.50 for ages 9-12, and free for those under nine years of age. Cabin costs per night are double the camping costs ($10.00 for 13 years or older, $5.00 for ages 9-12). For more information on the event or accommodations, cal Dave Enger at 425-822-6254.

The Washington State Orienteering Association will meet on Saturday, May 3 at 4:00 PM at Kent Verbeck's home located between Easton and Cle Elum, Washington. The meeting will follow the Bullfrog Pond event earlier in the day. Kent will prepare one of his famous spaghetti feeds for meeting attendees. For more information about the meeting, call Kent Verbeck at 509-674-2755 or Dave Enger at 425-822-6254.

State Championships an A meet. On August 23-24, Cascade OC will host a two day A meet at Trollhaugen (please see entry form in the July newsletter). The designation "A meet" means that the event will be organized to the standards of the US Orienteering Federation, and will include courses for all levels of experience, from novice to elite. For members of Washington State O' clubs, the event will serve as the 1997 state championships.

The format of the weekend event will be the same as that to be used in August 1998, when Cascade OC will host the US Championships. The first day will be a "normal" set of courses, and the individual results for the weekend will be determined by them. The second day will be a four-person relay, and will be the state relay championships. The courses that form the four laps of the relay will range from a beginner course to short advanced.

Eligible relay teams must have at least four "points" as follows: participants 35 years old or older earn 1 point, 50 and older earn 2 points, 65 and older earn 3 points, 20 and younger earn 1 point, 16 and younger earn 2 points, and 14 and younger earn 3 points (age is based on the participant's age as of December 31, 1997); women contribute an additional 2 points. Teams with 4 points comprised of four different members of the same Washington State O' club will be eligible for the state relay championship; awards will be given to other 4 point teams. Participants are welcome to form teams that do not have 4 points, and participants will be allowed to do more than one lap of the relay (e.g., two participants could do the laps as person A, person B, person A, person B), but these teams will not receive awards.
From I-90 exit 62, head southwest and west, following orange and white O' signs to the start/finish area. There will be very limited facilities.
For questions or to volunteer, please call Mike Schuh 206-783-3960.

This year on August 23/24, Cascade Orienteering Club is hosting an "A"-meet on the Trollhaugen map just off of I-90 some 62 miles east of Seattle. (Please see Meet Director Mike Schuh's article in this issue and the registration flyer at upcoming local events and the next issue of the Bearing.) COC is also hosting the annual United States Orienteering Federation Convention starting that Sunday afternoon, the 24th, and continuing through Tuesday the 26th.

Since many of you may not be familiar with what happens at a USOF convention or why it is an enjoyable and important event, I thought you might appreciate a little more info. (We will have all of the details in the next issue, but please feel free to give me a call; Rick at 367-2606 in Seattle, or visit our website http://weber.u.washington.edu/~bill3/conv97.html if you have any questions before then.)

Basically, the convention is a blend of three things: workshops, meetings/discussions, and social orienteering events and programs. The two days are also dynamic and fun. So whether you are involved with helping run your club, or a meet, or would just like to run better at a meet, you will find the convention the "place to be".

This year's "formal" workshops will have a broad range from GPS for O Mapping to O First Aid 101 (ankles to Zinc Oxide) for example; they will also be mostly hands-on or interactive. The workshops will be offered Monday morning, Monday afternoon, and Tuesday morning. Moreover, because there are about three different workshops run concurrently, there is truly something for everyone. There will also be a guided hike or alternative activity during these periods. (For example, we have arranged to have the author of the climbing guide for the local area to come and give a brief talk and then host a session "on the rock" for those wishing to try "vertical O".)

Many come to the convention also to take an active part in USOF and attend some of committee meetings that really affect all of us. These range from rankings (the good, the bad, and the ugly) to environmental concerns (orienteering land access 101). And while these might sound somewhat daunting or dry, each host really goes out of his or her way to make everyone, especially newcomers, feel welcome and keep the meeting moving. So, here is your chance to help your club and USOF grow; moreover, you can really get the lowdown on what is what or likely to be such, as well as let the folks upstairs know what you think. (These shorter/smaller sessions are scheduled for late afternoon and early evening.)

There are also those who feel that the heart of the convention is the socializing with orienteers from around the nation. To promote that we will have both informal and "formal" social options. For example, there will be Peter Golde modified score-O events before lunch on Monday and Tuesday and the first ever "regional volleyball tournament" in the late afternoons. We will also have more "formal" social programs in the evenings. All of this, and more, will be detailed in the next issue of the Bearing: for those in training for the annual 2-mile run, the course this year is both flat, fast, and easy to follow (early odds makers put Mark Howlett at?. no wait, I'd better not say).

All this takes place about 34 miles east of Seattle at a scenic environmental camp just outside of North Bend. You can stay at the camp or commute from home or go up-scale and stay at a motel in the area. The camp has arranged a nice set of meals for us, too. (Besides, everyone knows that the really good times and info are had at the meals; did I mention door prizes.) Mostly, we think you will like the nice workshop and meeting rooms coupled with a rich trail network big enough for the score-O events right at camp.

OK, just wanted to give you a heads-up on why you really should take Monday the 25th and Tuesday the 26th off from work and plan on coming to the USOF Convention that is this year in our own backyard. So don't miss the fun, the fellowship, and the field sessions: save those dates; you will be glad you did.

St. Edward State Park On Saturday the 10th of May and Sunday the 8th of June, COC will be hosting work parties at St. Edward State Park, the home of our annual Bog Slog (a.k.a. O' Lang Syne) event. Our project for this year is to "attack the green". By that I mean we have been asked to help remove unwanted non-native vegetation. So, rather than building trail, this year we will be making the area more runable. (OK, the park isn't actually taking this tack for our benefit, but rather to keep the non-native vegetation in check. Say, are blackberries native?) The one the majority of us worked on was a small non-orienteering park but also one in the same system as both Hamlin and Boeing Creek. In short, the King County Parks Dept. was impressed with our work and, on top of the satisfaction we received for our efforts, the projects help foster/maintain a positive rapport with the Parks Office. Moran State Park Speaking of state parks, Rick Hood went to a meeting about user access regarding off-trail areas at Moran State Park, since we have a map of Moran and have experienced some access problems in the past. Basically, there are three issues: How orienteering is classified in the State Park's CAMP or Classification and Management Planning project; how the land at Moran is classified; and what precedent, if any, this has for other State Parks and Washington State public lands. (Rick spoke with three key individuals from the Department and followed up our concerns with a multi-page letter; he hopes to be able to report more positive new in the next Bearing. This is a potentially very major issue for all orienteers and we thus welcome your input and involvement.)

As you may know, come June 1st the US Forest Service will implement a "Trail Park" Pass System, very similar to the existing "SnoPark" System. The bad news is that one must pay $3 a car to park within a 1/4 mile of trailhead at selected locations or obtain a yearly pass for $25. The good news is that at least 80% of the monies generated must be used for maintenance of trails in that area. The better news is that if one is willing to volunteer two days doing trail work on USFS lands, one gets a pass for those work days plus a yearly pass for no cost. The best news for orienteers is that since we don't typically use a designated trailhead parking area, the Trail Park System only effects us if we are going hiking or some other non-orienteering activity.

What a wonderful day to orienteer. It was 70F and severely clear over the course at the confluence of the mighty Snake and Columbia Rivers. Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently adminis-tered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteer-ing is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protrac-tor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for loan (with deposit) at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense under-brush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair of socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!
